content_en:
Among the more practically oriented sections of Al-Muqaddimah are its discussions of craft production (sina'at), knowledge transmission, and the relationship between these activities and the development of urban civilization. These sections, which some modern economists and historians of technology have found particularly insightful, represent Ibn Khaldun's attempt to understand the material and intellectual conditions of advanced civilization.

Ibn Khaldun observed that craft skills (sina'at) are not simply technical capacities that individuals possess naturally but are complex traditions of accumulated knowledge that require sustained social conditions for their development and transmission. A craft tradition requires teachers, students, materials, markets, and the time and security to develop over generations. This means that craft skills are characteristically urban phenomena — they flourish in settled cities that provide the necessary conditions, and they deteriorate when cities are destroyed or depopulated.

He used this observation to explain a pattern he had noted in Islamic history: when the Mongols and other conquerors destroyed major cities, they did not merely eliminate buildings and populations but destroyed accumulated craft traditions that could not be quickly rebuilt. The decline of certain artistic and technical capabilities in the post-Mongol Islamic world, in his view, reflected the destruction of the social conditions within which those capabilities had been cultivated.

For knowledge transmission, Ibn Khaldun made similar observations. Intellectual traditions require chains of teacher-student transmission, specialized libraries, institutional settings, and critical masses of scholars in conversation with one another. When these conditions are disrupted — by conquest, plague, or political instability — intellectual traditions can be lost in ways that individual talent and determination cannot reverse.

His account of the different disciplines of Islamic learning, their institutional conditions, and their regional variations provides a remarkable sociological analysis of Islamic intellectual life — one of the earliest attempts to understand the social conditions of knowledge production.
